segments.s313-g217: به دست بداندیش is an instrumental prepositional phrase: به + دست = 'by the hand (of)'; بداندیش modifies دست as an attributive adjective 'ill‑wisher, malicious‑minded'. بر + کشته + شد is a classical/perhaps poetic passive verbal construction: کشته is the past participle 'killed/slain' and شد is the auxiliary 'was/became'; the particle بر precedes the participle here (an archaic/poetic placement) and gives the sense 'struck down / was slain'. Word order is typical of classical Persian poetry where prepositional/agent phrases can come before the passive verb.
segments.s313-g218: چنین modifies the following nominal phrase and here means 'thus' / 'such'; تخم کین is a compound noun 'seed (تخم) of vengeance/hatred (کین)' with تخم as head and کین as genitive/qualifier. در جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world' placed before the verb for emphasis. کشته شد repeats the passive participle + auxiliary 'was killed/was destroyed' (past passive); the repetition of the passive underlines the result and is a poetic device.
segments.s313-g41: نباید + که introduces an impersonal prohibition/clause: 'it must not be that...'. گردد is the present/subjunctive/past-form of گشت here meaning 'become' (i.e. 'become naked'); تنش is 'his body' (تن + enclitic -ش). Word order is somewhat inverted/poetic: the negation + subordinate clause (نباید که گردد) precedes the noun (تنش) which is the patient of 'become'.
segments.s313-g42: بر آن هم = 'upon that also' (بر = upon; آن = that; هم = also). نشان خسته is a noun phrase 'the worn/spent mark' (خسته here = 'worn, spent'), and در جوشنش places that mark 'in his cuirass' (جوشن + enclitic -ش = his armor). The copula/verb is omitted (poetic ellipsis), so the phrase functions as a locative/explanatory clause.
segments.s313-g43: یکی here is indefinite ('a certain' / 'they made a'), دخمه is 'tomb/sepulchre' and سازید is the past 3rd-plural 'they built/made'. مشک و گلاب are instruments or contents (the tomb was perfumed/filled with musk and rosewater); the fragrances follow the verb as typical in Persian.
segments.s313-g44: چنانچون ... بود introduces a comparative clause 'as/just as ... was'. بود is the past copula 'was'. شاه را جای خواب literally reads 'for the king a place of sleep' — شاه را marks the king as the beneficiary/possessor in an older/poetic order (instead of جای خوابِ شاه).
segments.s313-g45: بپوشید is past 3rd-plural 'they covered/they put on'. رویش = روی + -ش = 'his face'. به marks instrument/material ('with'), so به دیبای چین = 'with brocade of folds' i.e. 'pleated brocade' (چین = folds/pleats) modifying دیبا.
segments.s313-g46: که introduces an explanatory/correlative clause ('for/because'). مرگ بزرگان بود همچنین = 'such was the death of the great ones' with بود as the copula (past) and همچنین 'likewise/so' emphasizing that this is the proper manner for the death of nobles.
segments.s313-g47: و زآن انجمن = 'and from that assembly/gathering' (زآن = 'from that'). کشته شد is the passive 'were killed/was slain' (کشته = slain; شد functioning as passive auxiliary), and ده هزار follows as the quantified subject 'ten thousand' (poetic inversion places the number after the verb).
segments.s313-g48: This is the list of who were slain: سواران = 'horsemen/riders' and گردان خنجرگزار = 'troops/companies of dagger-bearers' (خنجرگزار is a compound noun describing those who carry daggers). The noun phrase follows the verb as the victims counted by 'ten thousand.'
segments.s313-g89: بفرمود is an honorific/past reporting verb ‘he commanded/said’. The preposition به marks the indirect object/recipient ('to'), so به اشکش = 'to Ashkash' (recipient). تا begins a subordinate purpose/result clause ('so that / that'), and با سپاه ('with the army') is an adjunct indicating accompaniment—word order is: recipient + reporting verb, then the subordinate clause introduced by تا which continues in the next segment.
segments.s313-g90: This is the content of the تا-clause: به مکران = 'to Makran' (به as goal/‘to’), بباشد is a 3rd-person optative/subjunctive/present-form of بودن used here to express 'may he be' / 'that he be' (reported command), and یکی چندگاه is an adverbial phrase 'for a while' (literal: 'one [i.e. a single] some-time'). Together: 'that he be in Makran with the army for a while.'
segments.s313-g91: نجوید is a 3rd-person form of جستن with the negative prefix ن-, idiomatically giving 'he sought nothing' (i.e. 'he did not seek [anything] except ...'). جز (except) combined with از introduces the limited objects: 'جز از خوبی و راستی' = 'except/from goodness and truth'—so the clause means 'he sought nothing except goodness and truth.'
segments.s313-g92: نیارد here is an archaic/poetic negative verb form meaning 'he did not bring' / 'he did not allow' (i.e. 'there was no bringing of...'). به کار اندرون = 'into the inner work/affairs' (به marks direction or target), and کاستی is the object 'deficiency/lack'. The whole clause thus says he did not admit any deficiency into the internal affairs/administration.
segments.s313-g93: زآن شهر is an ablative phrase 'from that city' (ز = from + آن = that). راه بیابان گرفت is a common classical construction: گرفت (past of گرفتن) = 'took' and راه بیابان = 'the road of the desert' i.e. 'took to the desert/wilderness'. Word order places the source first, then the verb 'took' at the end.
segments.s313-g94: همه رنجها is the direct object 'all hardships/sufferings'. بر دل is a locative/figurative phrase 'upon the heart' indicating emotional bearing, and آسان modifies گرفت: آسان گرفت = 'took [them] easily' or 'bore [them] lightly'. گرفت is again past tense 'he took/assumed.' The clause means he bore all hardships lightly in his heart.
segments.s313-g95: چنان شد = 'thus it became/so it was' (an outcome-stating phrase). به here functions as an agent/instrument marker 'by' (به فرمان = 'by the command of'), followed by یزدان پاک 'the pure God' (noun + adjective). The whole prepositional phrase attributes the event to divine command.
segments.s313-g96: که introduces a result clause 'so that/that'. اندر بیابان = 'in the desert', and ندیدند is past 3rd person plural 'they did not see'. خاک is the direct object 'earth/dust'—poetically the clause states that, by that command, 'they did not see any earth in the desert.'
segments.s313-g9: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past ('you said'); Persian often places the subject pronoun optionally before the verb for emphasis. برآمد is a past intransitive 'arose/rose up' and takes زمین (the logical subject) after it here (verb–subject order). بآسمان is the prefixed prepositional phrase 'to/into the sky' (ب- + آسمان) indicating direction.
segments.s313-g10: وگر is a conditional/concessive 'and if' (classical/poetic). گشت is the past/simple perfect sense 'became/turned' with the theme moved before its subject (verb then noun): خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. اندر نهان is a classical prepositional phrase 'in/within hidden', i.e. 'hidden away'. The clause is linked to the previous by وگر to give a conditional or concessive sense.
segments.s313-g11: طلایه (vanguard) is the subject and بیامد is the simple past 'came' (subject before verb here). به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the king' with به + noun (نزدیک) indicating proximity toward the king.
segments.s313-g12: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that/for'), here giving the result or circumstance. مکران is the clause subject; سیه شد is 'became black/dark' (شد = became). ز + گرد here uses the poetic z (از) meaning 'from/by' and گرد is used as 'dust' (گردِ سپاه = the dust of the army), so the whole phrase means 'Makran became dark/black from the army's dust.'
segments.s313-g13: همه روی کشور is a nominal phrase 'the whole face/surface of the country' (همه = whole/entire + روی = face/surface + کشور = country). درفشست و پیل are nouns listed as what fills that face — 'banners (standards) and elephants' — in a classical nominative/nominal sentence where the copula is omitted and the predicate is a nominal phrase.
segments.s313-g14: ببیند is a 3rd-person present/habitual or narrative present 'he sees' (the prefix ب- + بیند form). کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. شهریار is the subject 'the king'. از دو میل is a prepositional phrase of distance 'from two miles (away)'; میل here denotes distance (not listed among the provided senses), so the sense for میل is not available in the sense list.
segments.s313-g15: بفرمود is an honorific past 'he commanded/said' (polite third person). تا here is the subordinating particle meaning 'so that / in order that' introducing purpose/result. برکشیدند is past plural 'they raised/drew up' and صف is the object 'the row/ battle-line' — together: 'he ordered that they draw up the ranks.'
segments.s313-g16: گرفتند is past plural 'they took/seized'. The direct objects follow (گوپال و خنجر); به کف is an adverbial phrase 'into/by/with (their) hand(s)' (به + کف, where کف = palm/hand). The precise identity of گوپال is uncertain from the provided sense list, so its sense is left unspecified here.
segments.s313-g25: Literal word order is subject (دو لشکر) + adverbial phrase (بر آن گونه) + object/phrase (صف) + verb (برکشید). برکشید is a past-tense, third-person singular verb here meaning 'raised/drew up' and attaches to صف ('battle-line') — i.e., 'drew up a line.' The prepositional phrase بر آن گونه (= 'in that manner') modifies how they formed the lines.
segments.s313-g26: که introduces a result/clause ('so that/that'). شد is the past/synoptic of شدن meaning 'became/was made.' از گرد is an ablative phrase 'from/out of dust' where گرد means 'dust' (a common poetic usage). ناپدید here describes the resulting state of آسمان ('the sky') — 'the sky became invisible/was obscured.'
segments.s313-g27: Order: noun (سپاه, 'the host') + locative/verb cluster (اندر آمد, 'came in/entered') followed by a descriptive phrase (دو رویه چو کوه). چو introduces a simile ('like/as'), so دو رویه here describes the formation — 'in twofold/ two-sided fashion' — compared to a mountain (کوه). آمد is simple past 'came'.
segments.s313-g28: رده برکشیدند is past tense, third-person plural 'they drew/raised ranks' (برکشیدند = they raised/formed). هر دو گروه ('each/both groups') is the subject performing that action; Persian places the verb at the end here, typical of older/poetic word order.
segments.s313-g29: به قلب اندر آمد is a prepositional/locative phrase ('into the heart/midst came') followed by آمد (past 'came'). سپهدار طوس is the apposition/noun phrase 'the commander Tous' — سپهدار is a title 'commander' and طوس is the proper name; Persian places the verb before the subject in this poetic line.
segments.s313-g30: جهان شد پر از is a copular/result clause: جهان شد ('the world became') + پر از ('full of') followed by the genitive list of sounds. ناله بوق و کوس is a noun phrase meaning 'the lament/cry of horn and drum.' The verb شد is past 'became' and links subject (جهان) to its resulting state (پر از ...).
segments.s313-g31: This segment strings together locative/time/position words: به پیش اندرون ('at the front/forward, inside') followed by کاویانی درفش ('the Kayanian banner/standard'). The noun درفش ('banner') follows its modifiers, and کاویانی qualifies it as Kayanian; word order places the location before the nominal phrase in this poetic register.
segments.s313-g32: پس پشت گردان is a compact phrase meaning 'after/behind, the rear troop' — پس/پشت indicate 'behind/after' and گردان here is a noun 'troop/unit' (not the 'revolving' sense). زرینه کفش is a noun phrase 'golden shoes/boots' modifying that troop (golden-shod). The order places the descriptor after the head noun, typical in Persian compounds in poetry.
segments.s313-g49: هزار و صد و چل is a classical Persian numeral built by joining thousand + hundred + forty with و ('and'); it reads 1000 + 100 + 40 = 1,140. گرفتـار شد is a passive/perfective clause: گرفتـار (caught/trapped) + شد (‘became/was’), so together 'were taken captive' or 'became captured.' Word order is Number + predicate.
segments.s313-g50: سر زندگان is a genitive phrase 'the heads of the living' (سر = 'head' + زندگان = 'the living'). پر ز تیمار شد is a stative result: پر z = 'full of' (پر ز تیمار = 'full of care') + شد 'became/was', so 'the heads of the living were filled with care.' Note ز here is the poetic/prepositional 'of/because of.'
segments.s313-g51: ببردند is the past, 3rd-person plural 'they carried/took away.' پیلان is the plural of پیل ('elephant'), so 'elephants.' آن خواسته literally 'that desired (thing)' = 'the booty they wanted' (آن = 'that/the', خواسته = 'desired/that which was wanted').
segments.s313-g52: سراپرده is a compound noun meaning 'inner chamber' or 'behind-the-curtain apartment.' The sequence '... و گاه آراسته' links another noun phrase: گاه here functions as a noun meaning 'place/seat' (classical usage) and آراسته is an adjectival past participle 'adorned/decorated.' Together: 'the inner chamber and the adorned place.' (Note: the supplied sense-list for گاه gave only temporal meanings; here it is used spatially—hence not matched there.)
segments.s313-g53: بزرگان ایران is a nominative noun phrase 'the great ones of Iran' (Buzurgān = 'great men'). توانگر شدند is a verbal phrase where توانگر is an adjective/noun 'wealthy' and شدند is the 3rd-person plural past of شدن 'became,' so 'became wealthy.'
segments.s313-g54: بسی نیز is an adverbial phrase; بسی in classical Persian means 'many/much' (here 'many'), and نیز = 'also/likewise.' با تخت و افسر شدند is 'they became with throne and helmet' — i.e. 'many also acquired thrones and helmets.' تخت = 'throne' and افسر here is 'helmet' (classical epic vocabulary). شدند is again 'became/obtained.'
segments.s313-g55: از آن پس is an idiom meaning 'from that time on' (از = 'from', آن = 'that', پس = 'after/then'). دلیران پرخاشجوی is a compound nominal phrase: دلیران = 'the brave ones' (plural) and پرخاشجوی = 'aggressive/quarrelsome' modifying them.
segments.s313-g56: به تاراج مکران نهادند روی is an idiom: به = 'toward/into', تاراج = 'pillage/plundering' (object), مکران = the Makran region, and نهادند + روی = 'they set (their) face' i.e. 'they turned toward' or 'set out for' (نهادند here = verb 'they placed/set', used in the idiom 'روی نهادن' = 'to direct one's face/aim toward').
segments.s313-g153: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. خسرو is the subject (proper name). ز is the archaic/poetic preposition 'from' (short form of از) and دریا is its source 'sea'; به خشکی is a goal phrase 'to the dry land/shore' and رسید is the simple past verb 'reached/arrived' (3rd sg). Word order is Subject + source/goal phrase + verb, typical for older/poetic Persian.
segments.s313-g154: This line contains two coordinated past actions: نگه کرد 'he fixed his gaze / looked' (نگه as 'gaze' + کرد as the light verb 'did/looked') followed by هامون جهان را بدید 'he saw the Hamun of the world.' جهان را is marked by the definite/object marker را, making 'the world' definite and part of the object phrase; syntactically هامون (the plain) modifies/stands with جهان را to yield 'the world's Hamun.' بدید is the past 3rd sg 'saw.'
segments.s313-g155: بیامد is the simple past 'came' (3rd sg); the subject is implied (Khosrow). به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into the presence of / before' and جهان‌آفرین ('world‑creator') is the addressee/title — i.e., 'he came into the presence of the Creator.' This is a standard verb + prepositional phrase construction.
segments.s313-g156: بمالید is the past form of مالیدن 'to rub/press' (3rd sg). The sequence بر خاک رخ بر زمین uses two locatives: بر خاک ('upon the dust/earth') and رخ بر زمین ('face upon the ground') — together meaning he laid/rubbed his face down on the dust of the earth. The repeated بر is the usual preposition 'on/upon'.
segments.s313-g157: برآورد here is a past verb meaning 'hauled up / brought up' (3rd sg) — contextually 'he pulled the ship and skiff out.' کشتی و زورق are the direct objects (ship and small boat). ز آب is a source phrase 'from the water.' The verb precedes its objects in this poetic ordering but functions as a normal transitive past verb.
segments.s313-g158: This clause expresses suddenness/need: شتاب 'haste' is the noun, آمدش 'came to him' (literally 'haste came upon him' — آمد + enclitic ـش = 'to him'), بود is the past copula 'was', and جای شتاب 'a place/need for haste' is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'there was cause to hurry.' The structure is nominal + verbal idiom rather than a simple intransitive verb.
segments.s313-g159: بیابانش is 'the desert' with the enclitic ـش (here functioning like 'before him / to him' or as a detached object), and پیش آمد is the compound verb 'came before / appeared before' (پیش + آمد). The coordinating و links ریگ و دشت ('sand and plain'), listing the terrain that lies ahead. Poetic word order places the place phrase before the verb.
segments.s313-g160: تن‌آسان is a descriptive compound 'soft‑bodied / indulgent of the body' (i.e., not hardy). به ریگ is a directional phrase 'onto the sand', روان here is adjectival/adverbial 'moving / flowing' (i.e., 'in motion' or 'swiftly'), and برگذشت is the past verb 'passed by / traversed.' Together they mean 'though soft-bodied, he crossed over the sand and kept going.'
segments.s313-g81: ستمکارگان (plural) is the direct object 'the oppressors'; را is the definite object marker attached to it. کنم is first‑person singular from کردن ('I will do/I will make'), here meaning 'I will (cause to) cut/split'. به دو نیم is a prepositional/measure phrase 'into two halves' placed after the verb in the poetic word order.
segments.s313-g82: کسی کو ندارد ز دادار بیم is a relative clause modifying the preceding idea: کسی 'anyone' + کو (an archaic relative 'who') + ندارد 'does not have' (present/poetic narrative 'has no') + ز دادار 'of/from the Creator' + بیم 'fear'. Note the archaic relative کو (= 'who/that') and the poetic preposition ز for 'of/from'.
segments.s313-g83: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign'. سالی here means 'for a year' (duration). به مکران bماند: بماند (third person singular of ماندن) is used in the narrative sense 'remained/stayed' and the preposition به before a place indicates direction/location ('in/to Makran') in this poetic construction.
segments.s313-g84: ز هر جای 'from every place' (ز = 'from/of', هر جای = 'every place'). کشتی گران is a noun phrase 'heavy/large ships' (noun + adjective), را is the object marker, and بخواند (from خواندن) here means 'he called/summoned'—a transitive sense used to command or call people/ships.
segments.s313-g85: چو آمد بهار = 'when spring came' (چو = archaic 'when'). و links the next clause: زمین گشت سبز uses گشت ('became/turned') as the verb and سبز as the predicate adjective 'green'. The sequence is a typical poetic coordination of temporal clause + descriptive clause.
segments.s313-g86: همه کوه پر لاله و دشت سبز is a compact descriptive line with some ellipsis: همه modifies کوه ('all the mountains'); پر لاله = 'full of tulips' (پر = 'full (of)' placed before the noun phrase it fills); و links to دشت سبز ('and the plains (were) green'), where the copula is omitted as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s313-g87: چراگاه اسبان = 'grazing grounds of horses' (چراگاه = pasture, اسبان = 'horses'). و جای شکار = 'and places for hunting' (جای شکار = hunting‑ground). These are noun phrases listed in apposition to the seasonal scene.
segments.s313-g88: بیاراست باغ از گل و میوه‌دار: بیاراست (from آوردن/آراستن) is a past/resultative verbal form 'adorned' (here rendered passively/descriptionally 'was adorned'); باغ is the garden (object/subject in poetic inversion); از before a list means 'with' or 'full of' in this context (lit. 'from flowers and fruit‑bearing'), and میوه‌دار is an adjective 'fruit‑bearing/fruitful'. Note the verb–noun inversion (verb before its topic) common in poetry.
segments.s313-g185: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). بشمری is the present-subjunctive/imperative-like form of شمردن ('to count') used with اگر to set a hypothetical ('if you should count'). سر به سر is an idiomatic repetition (سر + به + سر) meaning 'head by head' or 'one by one' / 'all alike', and نیک و بد are nouns used in parallel ('the good and the bad') to mean 'everyone/things, good and bad alike'.
segments.s313-g186: فزون نیست is a predicative clause meaning 'is not more' (فزون used adjectivally 'greater/more' + نیست 'is not'). تا here marks the limit/extent ('as far as' / 'up to'). گنگ is used as a place-name here (Gанг), and فرسنگ صد gives the measure 'a hundred parasangs' (فرسنگ = parasang, صد = one hundred); the sequence places the limit/place after تا: 'does not exceed up to Gang a hundred parasangs.'
segments.s313-g187: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now' / 'presently'. تا here introduces a temporal clause ('when' / 'as'), and برآمد is the simple past 'emerged/rose' (third person), followed by ز + دریای آب ('from the sea of water') where ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and دریای is the genitive/construct form of دریا 'sea' governing آب 'water'.
segments.s313-g188: به is the preposition 'to/at' marking location or direction. گنگست is the fused sequence گنگ + است ('is [at] Geng') with the copula attached; grammatically this is a predication 'is at Geng'. با marks accompaniment 'with', مردم is 'people', and افراسیاب is the proper name Afrasiab (the leader with whom they are).
segments.s313-g189: از introduces the source/cause 'from / on the basis of'. آن is the demonstrative 'that'. آگهی here means 'news/information' that follows. شاد is an adjective 'happy/joyful' and شد is the past-tense verb 'became' (third person); شهریار is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb, a common poetic word order (V S).
segments.s313-g190: شد again is 'became' (here introducing the second result clause). آن رنجها 'those sufferings' is the subject; بر دلش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his heart' (بر = on/upon, دل = heart) with the enclitic ش 'his'. نیز 'also' links this clause to the prior joy, and خوار is a predicate/adjective meaning 'despised/insignificant'. The order is verb-subject-predicate typical of Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s313-g191: در آن مرزها is a locative phrase 'in those frontiers/borders' (در = in, آن = those, مرزها = frontiers). خلعت is the object 'robe of honour' and آراستند is the past-tense verb 'they adorned / they bestowed' (third person plural). The object precedes the verb (SOV/OSV flexibility) — 'they adorned (the place) with robes' / 'they bestowed robes'.
segments.s313-g192: پس is a conjunctive adverb 'then/thereafter'. اسب is the object 'horse(s)'. جهاندیدگان is a plural agentive/noun 'those who have seen the world' — contextually 'veterans' or seasoned warriors — and خواستند is the past-tense verb 'they requested/wanted' (third person plural). Word order here is typical: temporal adverb + object + subject + verb ('then horses the veterans asked for').
segments.s313-g97: This is two coordinated nominal phrases describing atmosphere and ground. 'هوا' (air) and 'زمین' (earth) are the heads, each followed by the predicative phrase 'پر ز X' where 'پر' (full) takes the preposition 'ز' (poetic 'از') and a noun. 'ببردند' is not present here — the clause is stative, with the adjective-like use of 'پر' forming 'full of ...'. Word order is head + predicate ('هوا پر ز ابر').
segments.s313-g98: 'جهانی' (the world/whole world) is the head noun followed by the same predicative pattern 'پر از ...' (full of). Note the use of 'از' (standard preposition) instead of the shorter poetic 'ز' above; 'لاله و شنبلید' are coordinated objects filling the world. The clause is descriptive and syntactically parallel to the first half.
segments.s313-g99: 'خورشهای مردم' is a genitive construction meaning 'the people's dishes/ stews' (خورشهای = plural + ezāfe-implied possessive). 'ببردند' is 3rd person plural past of بردن with the archaic/poetic prefixed b- (they brought/took), and 'پیش' is a postposed adverb/preposition meaning 'forward / before (someone)'. Together this yields 'they brought the people's stews forward / before (the court or assembly)'.
segments.s313-g100: This segment is a sequence of locative prepositional phrases modifying 'ببردند'. 'به گردون' uses گردون poetically for 'canopy/sky/heaven' (here: pavilion/roof); the repeated 'به' is a stylistic repetition of the preposition; 'زیر اندرون' literally 'under/within the interior' marks another location; 'گاومیش' (water‑buffalo) stands as the noun specifying either the hide or the object placed beneath. The overall effect lists where the dishes were taken.
segments.s313-g101: This is a nominal verbless clause (typical in Persian poetry) listing locations and attributes: 'به دشت اندرون' = 'in the inner/plain interior' with 'به' marking location; 'سبزه و جای خواب' are coordinated nouns ('green growth/pasture and sleeping places'). The nouns are presented as the scene's features; the copula is omitted as is common in descriptive lines.
segments.s313-g102: This repeats the descriptive pattern from earlier: 'هوا پر ز ابر' (air full of clouds) and 'زمین پر ز آب' (earth full of water). The adjective-like 'پر' with preposition 'ز/از' forms 'full of ...' and the coordinating 'و' links the two balanced phrases.
segments.s313-g103: 'چو آمد' is a standard poetic conjunction + past-tense verb (= 'when he/it came'). 'به نزدیک آب' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'near the water' ('نزدیک' as adverbial/locative). 'زره' (armor) here is placed at the end of the segment by poetic inversion and will link to the following verb 'گشادند' (they were loosened/opened), so expect 'زره' as the syntactic subject or affected noun.
segments.s313-g104: 'گشادند' is 3rd person plural past of the root گشادن, here meaning 'they opened/loosened' (poetic passive/mediopassive sense is common). 'گردان' (troop/host) is the subject; 'میان' ('in the middle') is adverbial; 'از گره' ('from the knot') is an ablative phrase indicating the point from which they parted. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: verb then subject/adverbials describing how the troop opened up.
segments.s313-g145: This is a verbless descriptive clause typical of epic Persian: 'یکی' (= 'one' / 'one of them') introduces a member of the group, then two noun phrases give likenesses. 'سر چو ماهی' = 'a head like a fish' (چو = 'like/as'), and 'تن چون نهنگ' = 'a body like a whale'. The copula (has/is) is omitted — the similes directly modify the nouns.
segments.s313-g146: Parallel construction to the previous half-line: 'یکی' introduces another specimen; 'پای چون گور' = 'legs/feet like a gūr (here the wild ass/onager sense)', and 'تن چون پلنگ' = 'a body like a leopard'. Again the copula is omitted and the similes (چون/چو) attach directly to the nouns.
segments.s313-g147: نمودی همی is an archaic/poetic compound: نمودی (showed) with همی gives an iterative/continuous sense — 'kept showing.' The string 'این بدان آن بدین' is elliptical and uses the prepositional prefix ب (short for به) attached to demonstratives: it means 'this to that, that to this' — i.e., they showed one thing to one person and another to the other. (The line is parallel and deliberately terse; a copied segmentation in the wordlist splits bound forms, so two entries there are irregular.)
segments.s313-g148: به دادار بر خواندند آفرین is a short clause: به دادار بر = 'to/for the Creator' (به ... دادار), خواندند is past plural 'they uttered/sang/called', and آفرین is the exclamation 'praise/bravo' (here 'praise' directed at the Creator). The verb is explicit here (خواندند) and governs the acclamation.
segments.s313-g149: به بخشایش کردگار سپهر is a prepositional phrase/agentive phrase: به here means 'by' (an instrumental/causal use), بخشایش = 'mercy/forgiveness' (or generous bestowal), کردگار = 'the Maker/Creator', and سپهر = 'the sky/heaven' (the noun being affected). Together they set the cause/background for what follows.
segments.s313-g150: This clause gives the effect: 'هوا شد خوش' — 'the air/atmosphere became pleasant' (شد = past of 'become'). The coordinate 'و' links the next clause: 'باد ننمود چهر' — literally 'the wind did not show (its) face' (ننمود is negative past of نمود 'to show', and چهر = 'face'). Poetically this means the wind did not appear/there was no wind.
segments.s313-g151: گذشتند is past plural 'they passed/they went by'. 'بر آب' = 'over/on the water' (route), and the second 'بر هفت ماه' gives duration 'for seven months' — the preposition بر is used twice in slightly different roles (locative/over and marking duration).
segments.s313-g152: که introduces a clause of result/explanation: 'بادی نکرد اندر ایشان نگاه' — literally 'no wind did (cast) a look upon them.' بادی here denotes 'a wind / breeze' and نکرد is the negated past verb 'did not do'; نگاه ('a look/glance') is the object. The idiom means there was no wind to trouble them — 'no wind touched them.'
segments.s313-g137: شگفت is an attributive adjective preceding the rest of the noun phrase ('a wondrous...'). اندر آن آب مانده is a literary locative + past participle construction ('that remained in that water'); مانده is a past participle modifying سپاه (the noun appears last). The whole phrase = 'the wondrous host that remained in that water.'
segments.s313-g138: نمودی is the 2nd‑person singular past of نمودن (‘you showed/pointed’). به انگشت is instrumental here ('with/by (your) finger') — به used instrumentally — and هر یک is the direct object 'each one'. The final به شاه is the indirect object 'to the king' (به = to/for).
segments.s313-g139: به آب اندرون places the scene 'into/within the water' (به = toward/into + اندرون = inside). شیر دیدند is a past plural clause 'they saw a lion' and the conjunction و links گاو ('and a cow'). دیدند is 3rd‑person plural past 'they saw'.
segments.s313-g140: همی is the archaic/imperfective particle indicating ongoing or habitual aspect ('was/ever'). داشتی here functions as a past/imperfective verb 'had/possessed' (an archaic form used in epic diction) and takes گاو as its subject; با introduces accompaniment ('with the lion') and تاو is a nominal complement 'radiance/glow.' The clause says the cow possessed a radiance together with the lion.
segments.s313-g141: همان is a demonstrative 'that/same' modifying مردم (here 'those people/them'); مویها is the plural 'their hairs'. چون introduces a simile 'like' and کمند is the comparison term 'a snare/lasso.' The clause reads 'those same people and their hairs were like a snare.'
segments.s313-g142: همه تن = 'their whole body/entire body' (همه = whole/every). پر از is a fixed phrase 'full of' (پر here = full (of) and از = of/from), followed by پشم 'wool'; چون گوسفند is the simile 'like a sheep.' Together: 'their entire bodies were full of wool like sheep.'
segments.s313-g143: گروهی is an indefinite quantifier 'some/a group (of)' modifying سران 'heads' (here 'some had heads...'). چون سر گاومیش is the comparative 'like the head of a water‑buffalo.' The structure describes a subset of the creatures: 'some had heads like a buffalo.'
segments.s313-g144: دو دست is 'two hands' (numeral + noun). از پس is a locative expression 'from/at the rear of' (از = from, پس = behind), here governing مردم (used as 'their bodies/people'); و links the clause to پای پیش 'feet in front' (پای = foot, پیش = before/in front). The whole describes limb placement: two hands at the back and feet to the front.
segments.s313-g33: هوا (w0) is the subject 'the air/sky'. پر ز + noun is a poetic way to say 'full of X' (here پر (w1) = 'full (of)'; ز (w2) = 'of/from'), so هوا پر ز پیکان شد = 'the air became full of arrows'. شد (w4) is the past-tense/resultative 'became' used here to state a change of state; the coordinated و ... و تیر repeats the idea (پر و تیر) by listing 'feather and arrow/shaft' as things filling space.
segments.s313-g34: جهان شد = 'the world became' with شد (w1) again as 'became'. به کردار is a fixed simile/phrase meaning 'in the manner of / like' (به = 'as/in', کردار = 'manner, likeness'), so جهان شد به کردار دریای قیر = 'the world became like a sea of tar'. دریای is the ezāfe form of دریا (w4) 'sea' modifying قیر (w5) 'tar/pitch'.
segments.s313-g35: به قلب اندرون places the action 'into the inner heart' (به + قلب 'to/into the heart', اندرون 'inner/within') and شاه مکران is the genitive/title 'the King of Makran'. بخست (w5) is a poetic past form meaning 'closed upon / seized / struck' the heart (literal sense in lexicon 'shut/closed'), here used figuratively: 'it seized/struck the king's inner heart.' Word order is typical Persian: indirect object/goal first, then the verb.
segments.s313-g36: وزآن = و + زآن 'and from that' (w0 = 'and from that'). خستگی جان او هم = 'his life’s exhaustion/weariness as well' where خستگی (w1) = 'fatigue', جان (w2) = 'life/soul', او (w3) = 'his', هم (w4) = 'also/too'. برست (w5) is used here in the sense 'departed/was taken away' (poetic past), so the whole clause says that from that fatigue his life also left him.
segments.s313-g37: یکی گفت شاها سرش را بریم is a direct speech clause. یکی (w0) = 'someone/a certain one' introduces the speaker. گفت (w1) is the verb 'said' (the dictionary senses listed are conversational nouns; here it functions as the past verb 'he said'). شاها (w2) is the vocative 'O king!'. سرش را بریم uses سر 'head' (w3) + the clitic ش (w4) 'his' and the object marker را (w5); بریم (w6) is the subjunctive/optative 'let us take/cut' (we should cut off). Word order is subject-verb + vocative + object + verb of intention.
segments.s313-g38: بدو گفت شاه = 'the king said to him' where بدو (w0) = 'to him', گفت (w1) = 'said' (again verb sense), شاه (w2) = 'the king'. اندر او ننگریم is a negative optative/decision: اندر (w3) 'on/upon', او (w4) 'him', ننگریم (w5) is the 1st-person-plural present subjunctive/volitive of نگرستن 'to look' with negation implied in the poetic sense here (the king replies that they will not regard/look at him). The clause is reported speech with the indirect object (to him) placed before گفت.
segments.s313-g39: سر شهریاران نبرد ز تن literally orders words 'head (سر) of the kings (شهریاران) [he] did not take/separate (نبرد) from the body (ز تن)'. In this archaic/poetic line نبرد functions as a negative or negated past meaning 'does not/ did not take away' (the lexicon entry also has 'battle', but here it's a verbal form in context). ز (w3) = 'from' and تن (w4) = 'body'; the phrase means 'he does not sever the heads of kings from their bodies.'
segments.s313-g40: مگر نیز از تخمه اهرمن is a restrictive/exceptive clause: مگر (w0) = 'except/unless', نیز (w1) = 'also/indeed', از (w2) = 'from', تخمه (w3) = 'offspring/seed' and اهرمن (w4) = 'Ahriman' (the demon). Together: 'except perhaps from the offspring of Ahriman' — a poetic qualification that only demon-seed would lose heads.
segments.s313-g209: که here introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('that' / 'who') tying this line to what precedes; این باره is a temporal/occasional phrase 'on this occasion'; شارستان پدر is a noun phrase in inverted, poetic order meaning 'the city's father' or 'the founder/ancestor of the city' (classical Persian allows such juxtaposition without a genitive particle).
segments.s313-g210: بدیدم is the simple past, first-person singular 'I saw'; برآورده is a participial/adjectival form (past participle of برآوردن in poetic usage) describing a raised/uplifted state; از is the preposition 'from/out of' used here in a simile-like phrase, ماه is the poetic 'moon' (often used to describe a beautiful or shining head/face) and سر is the noun 'head' — together برآورده از ماه سر = 'with his head raised like the moon.'
segments.s313-g211: سیاوش که opens a relative clause 'Siyavash, who...'; از here marks source/means ('by' / 'from') and connects فر یزدان پاک as an ablative/agent phrase — فر is the Zoroastrian/epic term for royal/divine glory, یزدان 'divine' qualifies it, and پاک 'pure' is an adjectival qualifier; together 'by/through the pure divine farr.'
segments.s313-g212: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; باره‌ای is an indefinite/partitive form 'an occasion/one such case' (the -ی giving an indefinite nuance); برکشید is past tense, 3rd person singular of برکشیدن 'to lift/raise' and here functions as a passive-like or middle event 'was raised/was lifted up' in context; از marks origin 'out of' and مغاک is 'the abyss' — 'thus was lifted out of the abyss.'
segments.s313-g213: This noun phrase begins with ستمگر 'oppressor/tyrant' qualified by بد 'evil/bad'; آن کو is an archaic relative 'the one who'; به + بد + آخت is a poetic construction: به + [evil] + آخت(ن) ('to join/mix with'), so به بد آخت دست literally 'joined his hand to evil' (i.e. put his hand to wicked deeds). Word order is compact and characteristic of epic/persuasive Persian.
segments.s313-g214: دل هر کس = 'the heart of every person' (دل + distributive هر کس); از + کشتن او indicates the action people recoil from ('from killing him'); بخست is the past of بستن used figuratively 'closed/shrank' — here 'every heart closed/recoiled (i.e. people were averse) from killing him.'
segments.s313-g215: بر آن باره = 'on/at that occasion'; بگریست is past tense 'he/it cried or lamented' but here used with سپاه (a collective noun) so the verb expresses the action of the host; یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/altogether' modifying the verb or the noun to mean 'the whole host' — together 'the entire host wept.'
segments.s313-g216: ز خون سیاوش 'for/from the blood of Siyavash' (ز marking cause/relation); که introduces a relative clause describing Siawush: بد here functions adverbially 'in a bad/tragic way' or as an intensifier for the injustice done, and بیگناه is the adjective 'innocent' — together 'Siyavash, who was grievously/most unjustly innocent.'
segments.s313-g1: فرستاده شاه (the king's messenger) is the subject phrase; چون is used as a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when/as' and precedes the verb. بازگشت is a past 3rd-person singular verb ('returned'); in Persian narrative the subject pronoun is omitted and the verb carries the tense.
segments.s313-g2: همه شهر مکران is a noun phrase 'the whole/entire city of Makran' (همه as 'whole/entire'). پرآواز functions adjectivally/adverbially 'loud-voiced; full of noise', and گشت is the past of گشتن used copularly to mean 'became' or 'was made'. Word order: topic → predicate (adjective + copula).
segments.s313-g3: زمین کوه تا کوه is a spatial expression 'the land from mountain to mountain' (تا = 'as far as/from ... to ...'). لشکر is the agent 'the army', and گرفت is the past tense of گرفتن meaning 'seized/took (possession of)'. Classical/poetic word order places the locative phrase before the verb.
segments.s313-g4: همه here means 'everyone/all'; تیز (literally 'sharp') is used nominally/adjectivally to imply sharp weapons or swiftness (poetic compression). و links the next phrase; مکران سپه = 'Makran's host/army' (genitive). برگرفت is past of برداشتن 'took up/raised' — so the sense is 'all took up (weapons) and Makran raised its army.'
segments.s313-g5: بیاورد is a past-narrative verb 'he brought' (classical form). پیلان is the plural of پیل 'elephants'; جنگی qualifies them as 'war' elephants (adjectival). دویست is the numeral 'two hundred' placed after for metrical/poetic order, giving 'two hundred war elephants'.
segments.s313-g6: تو گفتی که = 'you said that' with گفتی functioning as the 2nd-person past 'you said' (poetic). که introduces the reported clause. اندر زمین جای نیست = 'there is no place in/on the land' where اندر = 'in/within', جای = 'place', and نیست = negative copula 'is not'. Note that the provided lemma senses lacked the verb-sense for گفتی, so that item is marked accordingly.
segments.s313-g7: از here is causal/ablative 'from/because of', introducing causes: آواز اسبان = 'the sound/noise of the horses' and جوش سپاه = 'the surge/rush/tumult of the army'. The coordinated noun phrases give the reasons for what follows.
segments.s313-g8: همی functions as a classical adverbial intensifier ('even/indeed' in this context). ماه بر چرخ = 'the moon on/in the sky' (بر + چرخ). گم کرد راه is a compound where گم = 'lost' and کرد is the light verb 'did/made' (past), with راه = 'way' — together 'lost its way'. Classical word order permits the object/affected noun after the compound verb.
segments.s313-g113: This clause uses two parallel prepositional phrases with the verb گرفت (took up) at the end. بر + noun + گرفت is a poetic idiom meaning “took up / adopted” (literally “took onto / upon”). The repeated بر before بندگی and نیایش marks the two objects taken up; گرفت is a simple past (perfect) indicating the action was performed.
segments.s313-g114: Here ستایش (praise) is the noun object and را is the Persian definite/object marker attaching to جهان‌آفرین (the Creator). The verb گرفت again means “took up / began” — i.e., he began to praise the Creator. The word order (object marker را before the noun phrase 'ستایش گرفت') reflects an older/poetic phrasing where ‘took up praise of the Creator’ is expressed with را marking the definite object.
segments.s313-g115: همی + past verb (خواست) is a classical Persian imperfective/past-continuous: “he kept asking / he desired.” از marks the addressee/source of the petition (he petitioned/from the Creator). بلند is an epithet modifying کردگار (the Creator) — here 'lofty' or 'exalted.'
segments.s313-g116: کز is the relative/subordinating particle 'that / (so) that' introducing the content/result he asked for. آبش = 'his water(s)' (آب + enclitic ـش). به خشکی برد uses برد (past of بردن, 'to bring/carry') with به + خشکی = 'into / to the dry land'; بی‌گزند is an adverbial adjective 'unharmed'. Grammatically, this subordinate clause expresses the wished-for action (what he asked God to do).
segments.s313-g117: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' qualifying ساز ('means, instrument, arrangement' here in the sense 'means/arrangement of war'). The noun phrase ساز جنگ و سپاه (‘means/instrument of war and host/army’) is followed by ورا (an archaic postpositional clitic meaning 'to/for him' or 'came to him'), which indicates direction/beneficiary.
segments.s313-g118: This segment names who arrived or were concerned: بزرگان ایران ('the great ones / chiefs of Iran'). The conjunction و links the next word گاه ('time / appointed occasion'), and the following ورا (again 'to/for him') applies to the coordinated phrase — an archaic poetic ordering where the beneficiary/directional clitic follows the coordinated nouns.
segments.s313-g119: همی گفت is the iterative past ‘he kept saying’ (classical imperfective). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing the addressee; کردگار جهان is the addressee phrase 'Creator of the world' (noun + modifier). The verb گفت here introduces the quoted address.
segments.s313-g120: شناسنده is an active-participle noun 'knower' functioning as an epithet of the Creator. آشکار و نهان are coordinated adjectives 'the manifest and the hidden' governed by شناسنده (i.e., 'knower of the manifest and the hidden'). The word order places the participle before its objects, a common poetic structure.
segments.s313-g129: "به هفتم" is a temporal prepositional phrase meaning "on/at the seventh (month)"; "که" introduces a temporal/subordinate clause "when/that"; "نیمی گذشتی ز سال" is an archaic/poetic word order for "you/one had passed half of the year" (نیمی = "half", گذشتی = past tense "passed", ز = poetic "از" = "of/from").
segments.s313-g130: The verb "شدی" here is an older/poetic past form meaning "(it) became/turned" (third-person past in Ferdowsi's style) and is followed by predicate adjectives "کژ و بی‌راه" (= "askew/awry and off-course"); the subject "باد شمال" (the north wind) comes after these predicates, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s313-g131: "سر بادبان تیز" is a noun phrase in poetic order: سر ("top/tip") + بادبان ("sail") with the adjective تیز (here "sharp/pointed/keen") qualifying the sail-tip; "برگاشتی" is a past tense verb (archaic/poetic form of برگشودن/برگاشتن) meaning "you/it unfurled/raised/opened" (the object — the sail — is implicit).
segments.s313-g132: "چو" introduces a simile/temporal clause meaning "as/like/when"; "برق درخشنده" is a simile phrase "gleaming/flash-like lightning" modifying the manner of the action; "بگماشتی" is an archaic past tense with the b- prefix meaning "you/it set/placed/made" (here "set it shining").
segments.s313-g133: "به راهی" = "onto/in(to) a course/way" (prepositional phrase); "کشیدیش" is a compound/poetic past form meaning "drew/pulled him/it" (کشید + ـش enclitic object), and "موج مدد" (literally "wave of aid/help") is the subject that performs the action — poetic inversion places the goal phrase first.
segments.s313-g134: "که" introduces a result or explanation clause; "ملاح" is the helmsman/pilot (subject of the verb); "خواندش" is past tense "he called him" (خواند + ـش object); "فم الاسد" is an epithet/name applied by the helmsman (literally "mouth of the lion" used here as a nickname), placed after the verb as the name called out.
segments.s313-g135: "چنان خواست یزدان" is a main clause meaning "thus/so God willed" (چنان = "so/thus", خواست = past "willed/desired", یزدان = "God"); "که" introduces the subordinate/result clause whose subject begins with "باد هوا" ("the wind/air").
segments.s313-g136: "نشد" is the negative past "did not become/was not"; "کژ" is the predicate adjective "crooked/hostile/askew"; "با" here functions as "toward/against/with" in the sense of relation or opposition; "اختر پادشا" is a genitival/title phrase "the king's star" (poetic juxtaposition without an explicit ezafe), so the clause means the wind did not become adverse to the king's star.
segments.s313-g161: همه شهرها is the subject ('all the cities'). دید is the past-tense verb 'saw/gazed' (here functioning as 'gazed' or 'looked'); بر سان چین is a short adjectival/comparative phrase introduced by بر سان ('upon/like') with چین 'fold(s)' as the image. Word order is topical subject + verb + comparative phrase, a poetic arrangement common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s313-g162: زبانها is the noun 'tongues/languages' and functions as the thing being compared; به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner of / in the fashion of' (به used adverbially + کردار 'manner, conduct'), and مکران زمین 'the land of Makran' is the locus of the image. The whole phrase modifies or completes the visual image introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s313-g163: بدان is the compound به + آن 'in/at those'; شهرها is the locative/topic 'those cities'. در بیاسود is a predicate phrase where بیاسود is an adjective 'uneasy, unrested' and در marks the state; شاه is the subject 'the king'. Word order here is topical locative + predicate + subject (poetic inversion).
segments.s313-g164: خورش 'stew/food' is the object of خواست, the past-tense verb 'he wanted/desired'; چندی 'for a while' modifies خواست; ز بهر سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'for the sake of the army' (ز بهر = 'for/on account of/for the use of' + سپاه 'army'). The verb خواست is past simple and governs the following complements.
segments.s313-g165: سپرد is the past-tense verb 'handed over/entrusted'. آن زمین 'that land' is the direct object. گیو را marks the indirect object with را as the object/recipient marker (Giv being the beneficiary). شهریار is the subject 'the king' (poetic word-order places verb first).
segments.s313-g166: بدو 'to him' is the clitic/phrase marking the addressee; گفت is the past verb 'said' (the lexical entry in the list is nominal but here functions as the verb 'said'), introducing reported speech. بر خوردی از روزگار is an idiomatic verbal phrase: بر خوردی (from خوردن in the sense 'befall, happen to') + از روزگار 'by/from fate' — literally 'what has happened to you from fate'.
segments.s313-g167: درشتی مکن is an imperative negative: درشتی 'harshness/rudeness' as a noun and مکن 'do not do' (negative imperative of کردن). با گنهکار نیز places 'even/also with the sinner' as the complement — the particle نیز here adds the sense 'even/also'. The clause is a prohibition addressed to the addressee.
segments.s313-g168: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for/because'. بی‌رنج 'free from toil/trouble' is a predicative adjective and شد is the past-tense auxiliary 'became'. مردم 'people' is the subject; از گنج و چیز is the agent/cause phrase 'because of treasure and goods' (از marking source/reason). The clause gives the reason or consequence behind the earlier injunction.
segments.s313-g201: نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning 'one should not' or 'it is not proper to'. که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. باشید is the present/subjunctive 2nd-person plural/archaic polite form 'be' (here rendering 'you be / you are'); یک تن means 'one person' (i.e. 'alone'); به + شهر means 'in/into the city' with به used to indicate location/direction ('in' the city here). The whole segment sets up: 'you should not be a single person in the city...'.
segments.s313-g202: گر is the conditional 'if'. از رنج ('from/of suffering') gives the reason or cause ('because of hardship' or 'out of need'). یابد is the present/subjunctive/indicative root 'finds/gets' (here 'experiences' or 'comes upon'). پی مور بهر is a short prepositional phrase: پی 'after/following', مور 'ant', بهر 'for/toward' (here 'for the sake of/gain'). Together: 'if, because of hardship, he follows after an ant for gain.'
segments.s313-g203: چهانجوی is a compound noun 'world-seeker' or 'adventurer' (the agent). چون here functions temporally/aspectually as 'when' or 'as'. گنگ is an adjective modifying دژ ('the fortress'), here meaning 'indistinct/obscure' or 'muffled' in poetic description. را is the definite object marker attached to دژ. بدید is the simple past 'saw'. Word order is subject + temporal marker + modifier + object + verb: 'When the world‑seeker saw the (gloomy) fortress...'
segments.s313-g204: شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became' or 'happened'. از here means 'from' or 'out of' and introduces the following phrase that indicates source or circumstance. آب‌دیده modifies رخش ('his steed') and is an idiomatic compound meaning 'seasoned/experienced' (literally 'having tasted water'); رخ/رخش is 'his steed/warhorse' (possessive -sh implied in poetry), and ناپدید is the predicate adjective 'vanished/unseen'. Poetic word order places the predicate after the modifiers: 'his seasoned steed became vanished.'
segments.s313-g205: پیاده شد is a verbal phrase meaning 'he dismounted / came down on foot' (پیاده used adjectivally/adverbially with شد). از اسب marks the source 'from the horse'. و joins the next action. رخ بر زمین literally 'face upon the ground' — رخ (face) + بر (on/upon) + زمین (ground) — indicating he put his face to the earth (an act of prostration or humility). The sequence describes two consecutive actions: dismounting and prostrating.
segments.s313-g206: همی is an archaic narrative particle often used to form continuous or simple past narrative ('then / thereupon'). کرد is the past 'did/made' (here part of the verbal phrase 'کردن آفرین' = 'to offer praise'). بر + کردگار indicates the addressee/target 'toward the Creator', and آفرین is an exclamation/noun 'praise' (i.e. he praised or uttered 'praise' to God). Together: 'he then praised the Creator.'
segments.s313-g207: همی گفت is the narrative past 'he said' (همی giving the epic/habitual flavour and گفت the verb 'said'). کای is the vocative particle 'O' used to address someone. داور داد و پاک are two epithets in the vocative: داور 'Judge/arbiter' and داد و پاک 'just and pure' (داد here as the noun/adjective 'justice'/'just'). The phrase is a direct address: 'O just and pure Judge.'
segments.s313-g208: یکی at the start of a self-reference can mean 'one' / 'a certain' — here functioning like 'I am but a single' or 'a humble'. بنده‌ام is the first‑person copular/nominal form 'I am a servant' (bende- + -am 'I am'), used as a humble self-reference. دل پر از is the construction 'heart full of ...' where پر ('full') is followed by از + noun to indicate 'full of [fear]'. ترس و باک are two synonymous nouns 'fear and dread' joined by و. Together: 'I am (merely) a servant; my heart is full of fear and dread.'
segments.s313-g105: همه is a preposed quantifier 'all/every'. چاره سازان here functions as a compound noun ('chara-sāzān') meaning makers/agents who provide a solution; سازان is the plural agentive. دریا follows as a genitive/attributive noun 'of the sea' (i.e. 'the sea's ...'). به راه is an adverbial phrase 'onto/along the road' (به = toward/into + راه = way).
segments.s313-g106: ز introduces origin/place 'from'; repeated for each place (ز چین و ز مکران = 'from China and from Makran'). همی is the archaic/progressive particle indicating ongoing or habitual action. برد here is a past action 'took/led' (classical past of بردن) with شاه as the subject placed after the verb (verb–subject order is common in poetry).
segments.s313-g107: به خشکی marks location 'on/onto the dry land' (به + خشکی). بکرد is the past verb 'he did' and stands before the relative clause آنچه بایست کرد: آنچه = 'that which/what' and بایست is a modal 'must/ought'; کرد is the main verb repeated for emphasis — together they mean 'he did what had to be done.'
segments.s313-g108: چو is a subordinating particle 'when/as'. کشتی is the subject 'the ship'. به آب اندر (= به آب اندر) is an older poetic way to say 'into the water' (به = into/toward, اندر = in/within). افگند is the classical past 'threw/cast'. مرد is the object 'a man' placed after the verb — literal order: 'when the ship cast the man into the water.'
segments.s313-g109: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he ordered/declared'. تا introduces the subordinate/complement clause of command/purpose ('that/so that they ...'). توشه برداشتند is the clause 'they took provisions' with برداشتند as past plural 'they took/removed'.
segments.s313-g110: به یک ساله ره = 'on/for a one-year way' — یک ساله is an adjectival/durational phrase 'year-long' and ره = 'route/journey'. راه بگذاشتند is a common classical phrasing meaning 'they set out' or 'they departed on the road' (بگذاشتند here = 'they left/they set in motion').
segments.s313-g111: This is a string of epithets in apposition to the subject: جهاندار = 'world‑holder/sovereign'. نیک اختر is a compound epithet 'well-starred' (i.e., fortunate/auspicious). راه‌جوی is another epithet 'road-seeker' or 'traveler'. Adjectives/noun compounds like نیک اختر follow the head and form fixed poetic epithets.
segments.s313-g112: برفت is the classical past 'he departed/went away' (here the provided lemma/senses were for 'برف' but context makes it a verbal form; no matching sense in the supplied list). از لب آب = 'from the lip/brink of the water' (لب used as 'brink' here). با + noun phrase 'آب روی' is a poetic collocation meaning 'with a water‑face' — i.e. 'with his face wet from the water' (با = with, روی = face).
segments.s313-g73: گر is a conditional particle 'if'. ایدون is an archaic/poetic verb here meaning 'comes/happens' (introducing the hypothetical). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/which' and بیند is a 3rd-person present subjunctive/plain present 'he sees'. سر بی‌گناه is the object phrase 'the head (i.e. person) of the innocent' — the noun + adjective order is standard.
segments.s313-g74: ببخشد is a 3rd-person present/jussive of بخشیدن 'to forgive/ grant' (here rendered as 'pardon'). سزاوار باشد is a semi-impersonal phrase 'it would be fitting' with باشد as the subjunctive/copula. ز marks the agent in a poetic way ('by/from the king'), so ز شاه = 'by the king'.
segments.s313-g75: از ایشان 'from them' is a prepositional phrase indicating source. چو بشنید is inverted chronological order common in poetry: چو = 'when' and بشنید is past 'he heard'. فرخنده شاه is the epithet + noun 'the fortunate king' serving as the subject placed after the verb.
segments.s313-g76: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he ordered/said'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that/that'), and بانگ زد literally 'struck/sounded a cry' — بانگ 'a shout' (object) + زد 'sounded' (verb). بر سپاه is the prepositional phrase 'to/among the army' governed by زد.
segments.s313-g77: خروشی برآمد is a noun + past verb 'a roar arose/issued forth'. ز introduces the origin 'from', and پرده‌سرای is the place 'parde-sarā' (here rendered as stage/curtain-chamber) from which the sound came.
segments.s313-g78: که‌ای here introduces the direct utterance/cry that follows (poetic speech marker plus the vocative 'ای' implied). پهلوانان is the vocative 'O champions', فرخنده modifies them 'fortunate/auspicious', and رای is used as 'counsel/plan' — together an address praising their good counsel.
segments.s313-g79: از این پس‌گر is a compact conditional 'if after this / if from now on'. آید is the present/subjunctive 'there comes' and ز جایی 'from some place/anywhere' indicates source. خروش is the noun 'uproar/tumult' as the thing that might come.
segments.s313-g80: This segment is a coordinate list governed by the initial ز: 'from injustice and plunder and war and tumult.' The repeated و simply links the nouns; each noun is a source/reason for the possible uproar.
segments.s313-g121: This is a verbless nominal clause where نگهدار (keeper/guardian) functions as the predicative epithet and خشکی و دریا are its complements ("of land and sea"). توی at the end is a poetic/colloquial second‑person copula/echo ("thou art"). Word order places the epithet before the nouns it governs (adjective/noun phrase followed by the copula).
segments.s313-g122: Parallel to the previous segment: خدای ("god/lord") is a predicative/epithet governing the following names ثری و ثریا (the Pleiades). The final توی again supplies the address/coupla sense ("thou art"). The whole segment repeats and reinforces the vocative/predication.
segments.s313-g123: نگه‌دار is an imperative/hortatory call meaning "preserve/keep safe." جان و سپاه are the direct objects ("life and army"); مرا after them marks possession/association ("my"); Persian allows the possessive clitic or object marker after a coordinated noun phrase for emphasis.
segments.s313-g124: همان is a demonstrative/identifying modifier here ("that/the same"). تخت و گنج و کلاه form a coordinated list (throne, treasure, crown). The sequence مرا (again split into components in the data) attaches to the whole list to indicate possession ("of mine").
segments.s313-g125: پرآشوب is a prepositive adjective meaning "full of turmoil/tumultuous" modifying دریا; از آن گونه بود is a stative phrase meaning "was of that kind/so" (از ... گونه = "of such a manner"). بود is the past copula "was."
segments.s313-g126: کز introduces a subordinate clause of consequence/relative origin ("that/from which"). او refers back to the sea. کس here functions as an indefinite that in context yields the sense "no one" in combination with the verb; نرستی is the past form of رستن in the sense "to be saved/escape" (so here "was not saved/escaped"). ب + دان (بدان together) functions adverbially as "thereby/therefrom," and برشخود means "upon oneself/by oneself" (i.e. not saved by his own effort/pride).
segments.s313-g127: به شش ماه is a temporal phrase meaning "after/in six months" (به + duration). کشتی is the subject "the ship." برفتی is a past motion verb meaning "went forth/was launched" (archaic/poetic past form). به آب marks the destination "into/onto the water."
segments.s313-g128: کز او = "from it/whence" (referring to the ship or the water). ساختی is a past tense verb meaning "made/constructed" (here rendered generically as "made"); هر کسی "each person/everyone" is the agent, and جای خواب "place of sleep" = "sleeping place/bed." Word order moves the source phrase first (کز او) and the result after it.
segments.s313-g193: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he ordered/said'. تا here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when/until' introducing a subordinate clause. بازگشتند is the past plural 'they returned'. شاه (the king) is the subject of the following clause (its placement at the end is an example of poetic/inverted word order preparing the next verb).
segments.s313-g194: سوی + noun marks direction 'toward'. گنگ here is being used as a place-name/adjective for the fortress (a toponymic reading), so it does not take the literal adjective senses listed. دژ is the noun 'fortress'. رفت is the past tense 'he went'. با آن سپاه is an instrumental/companional phrase 'with that army' (با = 'with', آن = 'that', سپاه = 'army').
segments.s313-g195: بر آن‌سو که is a locative relative phrase: بر = 'on/upon', آن‌سو = 'that side', که introduces a relative clause 'where/that'. پور سیاوش is a name construction meaning 'the son (پور) of Siavash' (پور as a patronymic). براند is a third-person verb meaning 'drove'/'was driving' (classical narrative form used here to describe action in that place).
segments.s313-g196: ز is the poetic preposition 'because of/from'. بیداد here means 'injustice/oppression' and is the cause introduced by ز. مردم فراوان is a noun phrase 'many people' (مردم = people; فراوان = abundant/many). نماند is the past negative sense 'did not remain / were not left' (نماند literally 'was not left').
segments.s313-g197: سپه را بیاراست: سپه (army) is the definite object marked by را; بیاراست is a past verb 'he arrayed/arrayed well' (آراستن with the prefix بی‑ used poetically). و is the conjunction 'and'. روزی بداد here functions as 'he gave provisions / supplied a day's sustenance' — روزی in classical Persian often means 'provision/food' and بداد is a past-tense giving ('gave') in an older/poetic form; note these noun/verb senses are not the ones listed in the provided sense table, so they are treated as unmatched.
segments.s313-g198: ز یزدان indicates source 'from God'. نیکی دهش is a genitive chain meaning 'a gift/act of goodness' (نیکی = goodness; دهش = gift/grant). کرد یاد is a light-verb construction: کرد (did/made) + یاد (remembrance) = 'he remembered' or 'he made remembrance' (a common classical phrasing).
segments.s313-g199: همی + گفت is a frequentative/continuous past construction: همی adds the idea of habitual/ongoing action ('he used to / he would'), and گفت is the past 'said' (the provided lemma senses for گفت in the data are nominal, so the verbal sense is not listed there). هر کس که جوید بدی is a generic conditional/relative clause: هر کس = 'whoever', که introduces the clause, جوید is present/subjunctive 'seeks' (used as a general/habitual action), and بدی = 'evil'.
segments.s313-g200: بپیچد is a third-person present/subjunctive form 'will be twisted/whirled' (the prefix بـ here is the classical imperfective/optative marker). ز marks the agent/cause 'by/from'. باد is 'wind' (not the optative particle here). افره is being used as a nominal element (likely a specific poetic name for a wind or a kind of tree-wind); given the provided senses list (maple/tree) this is best treated as a lexical item referring to a particular wind/force in the poem. ایزدی is an adjective 'divine' modifying the preceding noun phrase. Together the phrase means 'whirled away by the divine Afreh-wind.'
segments.s313-g169: از این پس is a temporal phrase 'from this after' = 'from now on'. ندارم is first-person singular present (negative: 'I do not have'); here it governs an infinitival/verbal noun clause that follows (پرستش کنم). کسی را is the definite/partitive object ('anyone' with object marker را). به کس ('to any person') reinforces the object 'anyone' — together the clause means 'I have no one (whom) to (for) anyone' in the sense 'I have no one to worship (among men)'. The word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: the verb ندارم appears before the object phrase.
segments.s313-g170: پرستش (a deverbal noun 'worship') plus کنم (subjunctive/present of 'to do') forms an infinitival/verbal complement 'to worship' or 'that I worship'. پیش is a preposition/adverb 'before, in the presence of', and فریادرس is the addressee 'the Rescuer/one who answers cries'. Grammatically this segment completes the object of ندارم: 'I have no one to worship before the Rescuer' → idiomatically 'I will worship no one but the Rescuer.'
segments.s313-g171: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from the host/army' (ز is the poetic variant of از). یکی نامور is a noun phrase 'a certain famed one' (یکی = 'a certain/one', نامور = 'renowned'). برگزید is simple past third-person singular 'he chose/selected' and is the main verb of the clause.
segments.s313-g172: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'one' → 'who...'. گفتار هر کس = 'the speech/words of every person'. بداند is the present/subjunctive form 'he knows' used here in a general/habitual sense; شنید is the past 'he heard'. The sequence (بداند شنید) should be read as a fused meaning 'who, upon hearing anyone's words, understood them' — the clause indicates ability to comprehend what was heard.
segments.s313-g173: فرستاد is simple past 'he sent'. نزدیک شاهان here functions adverbially/indirect-object-like 'to the neighboring/kings nearby' (نزدیک qualifies where/to whom the message was sent). پیام is the direct object 'a message/tidings'. Word order is flexible in classical Persian; the recipient phrase appears between verb and object.
segments.s313-g174: که begins the reported-content clause 'that...'. هر کس که is a free relative 'whoever'. او is the pronoun 'he/him' (the addressee/authority referenced in the message) and is the object of جوید 'seeks' (جوید is present/subjunctive/present-tense form used for general statements: 'seeks'). آرام و کام are the two nouns 'peace/calm' and 'desire/fulfillment' that describe what is sought. Grammatically this is a subordinate clause meaning 'that whoever seeks peace and fulfillment from him...'.
segments.s313-g175: بیایند is the jussive/subjunctive form 'may/should they come' (here part of reported instruction/invitation in the message). خرم is an adjective 'joyful' modifying the (implicit) visitors. بدین is a prepositional compound 'to this' (written here as ب + دین); بارگاه is the destination 'court/palace'. Together: 'may they come joyfully to this court' (reported command/invitation).
segments.s313-g176: برفتند is past plural 'they went/they departed' (narrative result). یکسر is an adverb 'all at once/entirely' modifying the verb. به فرمان شاه is an agent/instrumental phrase 'by the king's command' (به + فرمان + شاه). The clause reports that, at the king's order, they went off together immediately.
segments.s313-g177: This clause uses یکی + negative-past verb to mean 'not one' or 'no one.' سر نپیچید is literal 'did not turn/bend the head' (ن- prefix + past پیچید = 'did not bend/turn'), a poetic way to say 'did not bow.' زان مهتران (زان = 'from/of those'; مهتران = 'the chiefs/elders') attaches the group being spoken about at the end.
segments.s313-g178: به درگاه رفتند is a straightforward past plural action: به + noun (درگاه, 'court/presence') + رفتند ('they went'). چون here is comparative 'like/as,' and کهتران (an archaic/plural form meaning 'the lesser/younger ones') completes the comparison: 'they went into the court like lesser men.'
segments.s313-g179: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' دیدار here is the noun 'meeting/sight' (the occasion of seeing/meeting), and بد is an archaic/poetic element attached in the clause (here intensifying/qualifying the event); the main verb is بنواختشان (past, stem نواختن with object suffix شان) meaning 'he played for them' or 'he struck/saluted them.' Grammatically this is a subordinate temporal clause setting when the main action happens.
segments.s313-g180: به خورشید gives a direction/goal 'toward the sun' (به = to/at, خورشید = sun). گردن برافراختشان is a past transitive verb phrase: برافراخت (raise aloft) + شان (their) = 'he raised their necks' i.e. 'he made them lift their heads' (a figurative image of honor). The clause follows the temporal clause and describes the king's honoring action.
segments.s313-g181: پس (then) + از (from) introduces the source: گنگ دژ 'the mute/silent fortress' (گنگ = 'mute/insensible', دژ = 'fortress'). باز جست is a poetic past-tense verb cluster: باز = 'again' and جست (past of جهیدن) = 'leapt/sprang' — together 'leapt forth again.' آگهی is the noun 'news/information/announcement' and is the thing that 'sprang forth.'
segments.s313-g182: ز (poetic from/about) introduces the topics of the news: ز افراسیاب 'about Afrasiab' و ز تخت مهی 'and about the splendid/majestic throne' (تخت = throne; مهی is a poetic adjective 'moon-like/beautiful,' here an honorific epithet for the throne). This is a simple prepositional series giving the content of the آگهی.
segments.s313-g183: چنین 'thus' opens the quotation or reported speech; گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' (classical texts often place گفت before the speaker phrase). گوینده‌ای is indefinite 'a certain speaker' (ایه ending making 'one of...'), and زان گروه ('from that group') identifies his origin: 'Thus said one speaker from that company.'
segments.s313-g184: که introduces the content of what was said (a reported clause). ایدر = 'here'; آبست is an archaic contracted form effectively equivalent to 'آب است' (there is water / 'is water') — in this negative context it participates in a neither...nor construction (نه ... نه ...). پیشت = 'in front of you' or 'ahead of you'; کوه = 'mountain.' The full clause means 'that here there is neither water before you nor a mountain.'
segments.s313-g65: بفرمود is a past/honorific narrative verb meaning “he commanded/said” (here third-person). تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result (‘so that / in order that’). اشکش is the noun اشک with the possessive suffix -ش (‘his tear / his sorrow’); it functions as the subject of the following predicate and is modified postpositively by the adjective phrase تیز هوش (a two-word compound meaning “sharp‑minded / keenly aware”).
segments.s313-g66: بیارامد is a past narrative form meaning roughly ‘became (un)settled / was (not) appeased’ (here used as the main predicate of the تا-clause). از introduces the cause or domain ‘from / because of’. The three nouns غارت و جنگ و جوش are coordinated by و and list the things producing the state: ‘plunder, war, and جوش (here: ardour/excitement).’ Grammatically the clause says that the preceding noun-phrase (اشکش تیز هوش) undergoes the change expressed by بیارامد with the listed causes following از.
segments.s313-g67: کسی را نماند is a negative existential construction: کسی ‘someone/anyone’ + را (a topical/definite marker) + نماند (past of ماندن) = ‘there remained no one’. که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ‘who…’. زشتی is a noun meaning moral vileness/evil deed and کند is the present/habitual/optative form of کردن used here to report typical or potential action (‘would do’ / ‘might do’).
segments.s313-g68: وگر is a conditional particle (‘and if / but if’). با is the preposition ‘with / by’. نژندی (baseness, disgrace) and درشتی (rudeness, roughness) are nouns; together they form an instrumental phrase (با نژندی درشتی) modifying کند — ‘he would do (it) with baseness and crudeness’. The final کند repeats the verbal form used for potential/habitual action.
segments.s313-g69: از آن شهر is a locative prepositional phrase ‘from that city’. هر کس که is an indefinite relative (‘whoever, any person who…’). بد پارسا is an adjectival compound where بد (‘bad’) modifies پارسا (‘pious’), i.e. ‘impious / ill‑pious’ describing the people who then perform the action in the next segment.
segments.s313-g70: به + پوزش + بیامد is a verbal phrase: به پوزش ‘for pardon / to ask forgiveness’ + بیامد (past tense ‘came’) → ‘came to ask pardon’. بر پادشا places the motion/appearance before the king: بر with the person and پادشا ‘king’ = ‘before/to the king’. In older/narrative Persian the order places the purpose (به پوزش) before the verb and the addressee after it.
segments.s313-g71: که introduces reported speech/plea ‘that’. ما is the 1st‑person plural pronoun ‘we’. بیگناهیم is a present‑tense copula phrase ‘we are innocent’ (بیگناه + ایم). و links to بیچاره‌ایم, another copular phrase meaning ‘we are helpless/unfortunate’. Both predicates express the petitioners’ self‑description.
segments.s313-g72: همیشه ‘always’ sets a habitual/frequentive frame. به رنج is an ablative/experiential phrase ‘in/under suffering’. ستمکاره‌ایم is the substantive ستمکاره (one who wrongs/oppressor) with the 1st‑person plural copula ending — here the construction means they are constantly afflicted by oppression (literally ‘we are [subject to] the suffering of the oppressor’); syntactically the noun phrase follows the experiential preposition to indicate the source/condition of their suffering.
segments.s313-g57: خروش زنان is a genitive noun phrase where the head noun خروش (cry/uproar) precedes its modifier زنان (women) — classical Persian often places the possessor/modified noun after the head. خاست is an archaic past tense (3rd person sing.) meaning “arose/began.” از دشت و شهر is a prepositional phrase (from the plain and the city) that follows the verb.
segments.s313-g58: چشیدند is past 3rd-person plural 'they experienced/tasted.' زان ('from that' / 'because of that') refers back to the preceding events. رنج بسیار is a noun + postpositive modifier (رنج 'suffering' + بسیار 'very/much'), a typical Persian order where the intensifier follows the noun. بهر at the end is an archaic/prepositional particle meaning 'for' / 'because of' (giving the reason/purpose).
segments.s313-g59: به marks direction/target here ('to/into'); درهای شهر is the plural possessive 'the gates of the city' (درهای = gates). آتش اندر زدند is a poetic expression where اندر ('in/into') plus زدند ('they struck/struck at') yields the idiom 'set fire into' — i.e., 'they set fire to (the gates)'.
segments.s313-g60: همی here is an emphatic adverb ('indeed/altogether'). The clause places the subject آسمان (the sky/heaven) before the verb; بر زمین ('upon the earth') is the locus phrase with the preposition بر. برزدند is past plural 'they struck/beat' — in this poetic line it expresses a cataclysmic image ('heaven struck the earth' / 'heaven and earth were struck').
segments.s313-g61: بخستند is a literary/past verb here indicating violent action (contextually 'they struck/killed/shot'), though this particular lemma list does not show that sense. ز ایشان ('from/of them') gives the group affected; فراوان qualifies the quantity ('many'). به تیر functions instrumentally ('by/with arrows') — به + noun indicating the means.
segments.s313-g62: زن و کودک خرد is a coordinated noun phrase 'women and small children' where خرد ('small') modifies کودک (postpositive adjective). کردند اسیر is the verb + result phrase 'they made (them) captives' — اسیر is a predicative noun following کردن used to mean 'to take/make captive.'
segments.s313-g63: چو is the conjunction 'when.' کم شد is the compound past 'became less / diminished.' از آن انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'from that assembly' indicating the source/context of the feeling. خشم شاه ('the king's anger') is the noun (خشم) followed by its possessor (شاه); the predicate (کم شد) appears before the subject, a common word order in Persian poetry.
segments.s313-g64: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he commanded/ordered.' تا here introduces the subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that') expressing what he ordered. باز گردَد is the verbal phrase 'turn back / return' (باز = again; گردد is present/subjunctive 3rd sing. of گشتن/گردیدن), and سپاه (army) is the logical subject placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s313-g17: ز is the poetic equivalent of modern از meaning 'from'; مکران is the place (Makran) and طلایه is the subject 'vanguard' that performs the verb بیامد ('came', simple past). به دشت is a directional prepositional phrase 'into the plain' (به + place). Word order follows classical Persian: prepositional source + subject + verb + goal phrase.
segments.s313-g18: همه شب is an adverbial time phrase 'all night' (whole night). همی is an archaic/progressive particle marking continuous or repeated action ('kept / was continually'). گرد is an adverb/preposition 'around' and لشکر is its object 'the host/army'; بگشت is the verb (classical past of گشتن) meaning 'turned' or 'circled'. Together: 'it kept circling the host all night.'
segments.s313-g19: This is a noun phrase in apposition: نگهبان 'guardian' + لشکر از ایران 'of the army of Iran' (از here marks the genitive relation) followed by تخوار which functions as an appositive/name or epithet. The whole phrase identifies the person as the army's guard from Iran called/epitheted تخوار.
segments.s313-g20: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that'; بودی is the past-tense of بودن 'was'. به نزدیک او is a locative phrase 'near him' (به + نزدیک + pronoun). رزم خوار is a compound noun phrase where رزم (battle) modifies خوار ('despised'/'lowly') — here functioning as an epithet meaning 'one disgraced in/averse to battle' or 'a coward in battle'.
segments.s313-g21: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. برآویخت is a classical verb meaning 'rose up, sprang upon' (here 'leaped on/attacked'). با او 'with him' plus به هم ('at one another/together') marks the mutual/reciprocal action — he attacks and grapples with that person. The sequence is compact: 'he came and sprang upon him.'
segments.s313-g22: چو is the simile marker 'like/as'. The two nouns PIL and SHIR are compared with qualifying adjectives after them: پیل سرافراز 'a lofty/proud elephant' and شیر دژم 'a grim/sullen lion'. Classical Persian commonly places adjectives after the noun.
segments.s313-g23: بزد is past 'he struck' (classical preterite). تیغ is the instrument 'sword' (here understood as 'with the sword'). و simply links clauses. او را is the definite object 'him' (را marks the direct object). به دو نیم کرد is a resultative causative: به دو نیم 'into two halves' + کرد 'made/did' → 'he cut him in two' (literal 'made him into two halves').
segments.s313-g24: دل شاه مکران is a genitive chain 'the heart of the king of Makran' (possessive expressed by juxtaposition). پر از بیم کرد is a verbal phrase where پر 'full (of)' takes a following از + noun 'of fear' and کرد 'made' functions causatively — idiomatically 'his heart was filled with fear' (literally 'made the heart full of fear').
